title: Women and States: Norms and Hierarchies in International Society By Ann E. Towns Cambridge University Press. 2010. 249 pages. $34.00 paper
abstract: Ann Towns has set out to address important and underexplored theoretical and empirical issues in the field of international relations (IR). Focusing on state behavior in the context of international society, she takes a unique approach to examining how states make use of women by implementing practices that are not necessarily and primarily intended to advance the status of women, but to further the standing of the state in international society. Located within constructivist IR, her findings challenge the widely held notion that in the international arena, norms homogenize political life. She explores the shortcomings of that assumption and argues convincingly for its expansion to include the empirical truth that “norms [also] rank and set up relations of hierarchy among states.”(1) These hierarchies are significant because they are a fundamental part of international society, forming the basis from which change in state policy and practices emanates. Accordingly, norms and social hierarchies are closely linked: “norms necessarily draw upon and generate social ranking [defining states as] superior and inferior.”(185-86) In that sense, “states are not discrete and unrelated entities,”(186) but rather parts of what the late IR scholar John W. Burton (1972) described as a web of social relations, where “world society” includes other important relationships, such as international and nongovernmental institutions as well as the behavior and intersubjective meanings of individuals and groups who are involved in decision-making processes.
